coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 mesoporous film as an efficient electron transport layer for high-performance perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Deli
Zhang, Weifeng
Xie, Fengyan
Li, Yafeng
Abate, Antonio
Wei, Mingdeng
coredata.dc:description: In mesoscopic perovskite solar cells, the electron transport layer plays an important role in charge extraction and determines the charge-transport property to attain a superior photovoltaic performance. Herein, we report a low-cost, solution-based deposition procedure of decorating graphene quantum dots on the surface of mesoporous TiO2 film, expecting that a more effective electron-transport channel can be formed for the enhancement of solar cell performance, finally, an impressive power conversion efficiency of 20.45% is achieved in the resulting optimized perovskite solar cell. Furthermore, the results of various spectroscopic characterizations including electronic impedance spectroscopy, steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence spectrum elucidate that the introduction of GQDs on the surface of mesoporous TiO2 film not only enhance the electron extraction but also facilitate the charge transportation at interface between the perovskite and electron transport layer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Decoration
Electron extraction
Perovskite solar cell
Photovoltaic property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface decoration of mp-TiO2 by GQDs
Preparation of the spiro-OMeTAD solution and the gold layer
Characterization of GQDs/mp-TiO2 based PSC device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A typical TEM image of GQDs. The upper and lower insets represent the graphitic layers of one GQD under HRTEM and the photograph of GQDs solution.
XPS spectra of N 1s peaks of GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 substrates, respectively.

(a) The J-V curves of the PSCs which based on GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 films as the ETLs, and (b) The incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency spectra of the PSCs employing GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 films as the ETLs.
The best performing (η = 20.45%) perovskite solar cell based on GQD-1/mp-TiO2 ETL under simulated AM 1.5 solar irradiation. Solar cell performance parameters are given in the inset.
(a) A cross-sectional SEM image of the GQD-applied mesoscopic perovskite solar cell, and (b) the schematic representation of the prepared GQD-applied mesoscopic solar cells.

(a) The series resistance (Rs) obtained from impedance spectroscopy analysis from (b); (b) Nyquist plots of perovskite solar cells which were based on the GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 as ETLs, respectively, and the devices were measured at −0.7 V under dark conditions.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra (excitation at 406 nm) and (b) time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) (excitation at 406 nm and emission at 760 nm) of the bare perovskite film and on top of GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 substrates, respectively.
Photovoltaic parameters of the PSCs based on GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 ETLs.
EIS parameters of PSCs employing GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 films as ETLs, respectively.
Summary of fitted time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of each sample.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 mesoporous film as an efficient electron transport layer for high-performance perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Helmholtz-Zentrum Berlin für Materialien und Energie, Kekuléstrasse 5, Berlin, 12489, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Helmholtz-Zentrum Berlin für Materialien und Energie

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In mesoscopic perovskite solar cells, the electron transport layer plays an important role in charge extraction and determines the charge-transport property to attain a superior photovoltaic performance. Herein, we report a low-cost, solution-based deposition procedure of decorating graphene quantum dots on the surface of mesoporous TiO2 film, expecting that a more effective electron-transport channel can be formed for the enhancement of solar cell performance, finally, an impressive power conversion efficiency of 20.45% is achieved in the resulting optimized perovskite solar cell. Furthermore, the results of various spectroscopic characterizations including electronic impedance spectroscopy, steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence spectrum elucidate that the introduction of GQDs on the surface of mesoporous TiO2 film not only enhance the electron extraction but also facilitate the charge transportation at interface between the perovskite and electron transport layer.

Graphene quantum dots were used to decorate the mesoporous TiO2 film, which was employed as the electron-transporting layer for perovskite solar cells, resulting in a maximum PCE of 20.45%.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Photovoltaic (PV) cells that convert solar radiation into electricity are significantly attractive in meeting societal needs for providing renewable energy [1]. Among the various PV research communities, organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cells have attracted considerable interests because of their high power conversion efficiency (PCE) and low fabrication costs [2]. To date, the PCE of perovskite solar cells has been soared from 3.8% [3] to 22.7% [4] via a series of approaches such as the optimization of device architecture, morphology control of the perovskite film and surface engineering of the contact interface [5,6]. However, the photovoltaic performance of such PSCs is still far away from their theoretical prediction, moreover, the unstable properties of such devices also prevent their large-scale commercialization. And it is found that a main factor of limiting device performance is the energy loss, which is caused not only the barrier of the charge excitation from the photo-active layer but also the carrier recombination at interfaces occurring between the adjacent layers [7–9].

Hence, increasing efforts have focused on these aspects, for instance, by taking advantage of carbon derivatives such as carbon nanotube [10,11], fullerene [12], graphene [13], or graphene oxide [14], which have been used as additives or interlayer in PSCs, can apparently improve the morphology and crystallinity of the perovskite crystal film, provide an efficient charge-collection channel and facilitate electron extraction from perovskite layer to electron-transport layer. Agnese et al. incorporated a fullerene monolayers into the surface of mesoporous titania, and found that this strategy allowed a reduction of energy loss and facilitated the transportation of the carriers, the PCE of 11.7% was achieved [12]. Hadadian et al. prepared perovskite precursor solution with nitrogen-doped reduce graphene oxide as a passivating agent, resulting in the larger perovskite grain size, thicker perovskite layer and leading to an improvement of the PCE from 17.3% to 18.7% [15]. Wang et al. introduced a chemical modified graphene oxides into PSCs, which could be acted as an efficient interlayer between perovskite and hole-transport layer, and the functionalized graphene oxides could passivate the traps on perovskite films and benefit for hole extraction from perovskite layer to hole-transporting layer [16].

Comparing to the traditional graphene materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new type of graphene nanostructure, which exhibit good crystallinity and possess the same lattice structure like graphene [17]. Owing to the quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs can provide tunable band gaps and good chemical stability [18,19]. Therefore, GQDs have generated great interests in diverse types of photovoltaic devices, such as polymer solar cells [20], silicon solar cells [21], dye-sensitized solar cells [22] as well as PSCs. Yang et al. inserted an ultrathin GQDs layer between perovskite and ETL, and found a strong quenching of perovskite photo luminescence at 760 nm, highlighted that GQDs could provide a superfast electron tunnel for optoelectronic devices [23]. Jang et al. utilized O2 plasma treatment to incorporate GQDs onto blocking TiO2 layer, obtained a homogeneous and dense film which could boost the Jsc by ultra-fast electron extraction and improve the recombination resistance of the device [24]. Xie et al. reported that the electronic properties of SnO2 ETL could be enhanced though adding a small amount of GQDs, and average PCE of 19.2% was obtained which could be ascribed to the higher conductivity and lower electron traps in ETLs [25].

Herein, a facile and new strategy was adopted to decorate TiO2 mesoporous film with graphene quantum dots and a high efficiency of PSCs was attained. The GQDs/mp-TiO2 ETLs were prepared via a simple approach through spin-coating the GQDs ethanol solution on mesoporous TiO2 film and annealing in air at a low temperature (100 °C). Furthermore, the effects of GQDs’ presence on cell performance were also investigated. In particular, the short-circuit density (Jsc) of the PSCs was significantly enhanced when the GQDs were embedded on the surface of mesoporous TiO2 film. The increasing Jsc might be attributed to reduced series resistance of the devices and the superior electron extraction from the perovskite layer to mp-TiO2 ETL. Consequently, a maximum steady-state efficiency of 20.45% was achieved in the mesoscopic PSC based on the GQDs/mp-TiO2 as the electron-transporting layer.

All chemicals were analytical reagent and applied directly without any purification. Firstly, 0.3 g graphite was filled in the porous silica griddle. Then, the griddle was transported into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave with 2 mL of HNO3. After sealing at 160 °C for 5 h, the as-obtained sample was washed with 50 mL DI water. Finally, the transparent filtrate was transferred into dialysis bag for a week and the GQDs were dispersed in ethanol to prepare the graphene quantum dots solution (1 mg/mL).

First, fluorine doped tin oxide (F: SnO2) coated glass (FTO) was patterned by etching with 2 M HCl and Zn powder. The patterned FTO was cleaned by ultra-sonication and rinsed with detergent, deionized water, ethanol, acetone and isopropanol for 30 min, respectively. Then, the FTO was dried by clean air and treated with a UV-ozone machine for 15 min. The TiO2 blocking layer was prepared by spin-coating precursor solution of 0.15 M titanium diisopropoxide bis(acetylacetonate) (75 wt% in isopropanol, Alfa-Aesar) in 1-butanol (99.9%, Alfa-Aesar). After spin-coating, the substrates were annealed at 550 °C for 30 min and then they were left to cool down to room temperature. The mesoporous TiO2 (mp-TiO2) layer was deposited by spin coating for 30 s at 2000 r.p.m/min, using 25 nm particle home-made paste diluted in ethanol (0.1 g mL−1). Finally, the substrate was immediately dried at 150 °C for 30 min and sintered again at 550 °C for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface decoration of mp-TiO2 by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For depositing of GQDs, mp-TiO2 film was soaked with GQDs solution for 10 s before spin coating. After spin coating for 20 s at 2000 r.p.m/min, the substrate was heated at 100 °C for 30 min. Briefly, the first time that the GQDs solution spin-coated on the surface of mp-TiO2 was denoted as GQD-1/mp-TiO2, the GQDs solution spin-coated for the second time was denoted as GQD-2/mp-TiO2 and the mp-TiO2 without GQDs spin-coating was denoted as GQD-0/mp-TiO2, respectively.

The perovskite film was deposited from a precursor solution containing FAI (1 M), PbI2 (1.1 M), MABr (0.2 M) and PbBr2 (0.2 M) in a mixed solution of anhydrous DMF: DMSO (v:v = 4:1). The perovskite solution was spin-coated in a two-step program at 1000 and 6000 r.p.m for 10 s and 20 s, respectively. During the second step, 100 μL of chlorobenzene was dropped onto the spinning substrate 5 s prior the end of the program. Then, the substrate was annealed at 100 °C for 1 h in a nitrogen glove box.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the spiro-OMeTAD solution and the gold layer
ce:section.ce:para: After the perovskite annealing, the substrate was cooled down for a few of minutes. The hole transport material (HTM) was spin-coated onto perovskite film at 4000 r.p.m s−1 for 20 s. An HTM solution was prepared by dissolving 72.3 mg of (2,2′,7,7′-tetrakis (N,N-di-p-methoxyphenylamine)-9,9-spirobifluorene)(spiro-MeOTAD) in 1.0 mL of chlorobenzene, into which 28.8 μL of 4-tert-butylpyridine and 17.5 μL of Li-TFSI solution (520 mg Li-TFSI in 1 mL acetonitrile) were added. Finally, a gold electrode with a thickness of 80 nm was thermally evaporated on top of the device to finish the fabrication. The active area of the device was 0.09 cm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs/mp-TiO2 based PSC device
ce:section.ce:para: The photovoltaic performance of PSCs was recorded using a source meter (Keithley 2400). A PEC-L11 AM 1.5 solar simulator with a 1000 W Xe lamp and an AM 1.5 filter (Peccell) was applied as the light source (100 mW cm−2). J-V curves were measured at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra were collected using a PEC-S20 (Peccell) Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted employing an IM6 (Zahner). The impedance parameters were simulated by fitting of impedance spectrum through Z-view software.

The phase purity of the samples was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Rigaku Ultima IV using the Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a MUTILLAB 2000. The crystalline morphologies were examined by scanning electron microscope (Hitachi S-4800 instrument). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were obtained using a SPA-300 AFM (Bruker) and scanning range of the AFM images was 5 μm × 5 μm. Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) (excitation at 406 nm) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) (excitation at 406 nm and emission at 750 nm) were conducted with Edinburgn Instruments LTD (FLS 980).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by steaming the graphite utilizing a nitric acid vapor cutting strategy. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied to present the distribution and size of the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 . In particular, the GQDs derived from graphite exhibited an average diameter of 3–5 nm, and shown highly crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.216 nm which was consistent with that in the (100) plane of graphite (upper inset in Fig. 1) [26]. In the lower inset to Fig. 1, a photograph of the GQDs solution demonstrated that the GQDs could be well dispersed in ethanol with transparent and colorless appearance.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 substrates are shown in Fig. 2 to certify that the GQDs could be successfully embedded on the surface of the mp-TiO2 film. After inserting the GQDs, there was a typical peak (400.1 eV) emerging which was corresponding to N 1s, ascribing to the presence of nitrogenous functional groups binding to the obtained graphene quantum dots, while the film with TiO2 alone was lacked of N ligands, indicating that the GQDs have been successfully deposited on the surface of mesoporous TiO2 film. Meanwhile, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis and the corresponding elemental mapping of the GQD-1/mp-TiO2 film was carried out to demonstrate that the C element was indeed existed on the surface of the GQDs decorated mesoporous TiO2 film. Moreover, the spatial uniformity of the respective elemental distribution was observed, and it revealed that the elements Ti, O and C were homogenously distributed in the electron-transported layer (Fig. S1a-f).

Fig. S2(a-c) display the top-view scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the mesoporous TiO2 film with and without the decoration of GQDs. It is difficult to observe the GQDs appearing on the mesoporous TiO2 film due to their tiny size (≈3 nm) and ultra-thin thickness (sub-2 nm) [24]. Hence, atomic-force microscopy (AFM) analysis was used to investigate the surface morphology differences of mesoporous TiO2 films with and without the decoration of GQDs (Fig. S2(d-f)). Interestingly, the area root-mean-square (RMS) roughness values of the GQD-2/mp-TiO2 (19.7 nm) and GQD-1/mp-TiO2 (22.5 nm) were lower than that of the bare mesoporous TiO2 film. It is supposed that the pores and cracks of mesoporous TiO2 film could be efficiently infiltrated and covered through the decoration of the GQDs, resulting in the surface of the film becoming more homogeneously and densely. And more homogeneous and smooth GQD decorated-TiO2 film than that of the film with TiO2 alone led to superior contact with the perovskite film which could contribute to a decrease in the resistance and an increase in the shunt resistance of the PSC devices [27].

Fig. S3a compares the transmission spectra of FTO glass covered with the pristine mesoporous TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 films. It is found that the transmission of the GQD-1/mp-TiO2 was just slightly lower in the range of 360–550 nm, compared with that of the pristine mesoporous TiO2 film, which owned to the absorption of GQDs with a band gap of 2.4 eV [25]. However, the absorption strengths of the GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 based perovskite films are stronger ascribing to the assistance of the GQDs on the surface of the mesoporous TiO2 films (Fig. S3b).

To probe the morphology and crystal phase changes of the perovskite films deposited on the mesoporous TiO2 films which were decorated with and without the GQDs, the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis and X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed, and the results are shown in Fig. S4. XRD patterns of the perovskite films formed on GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 substrates are depicted in Fig. S4a. All diffraction peaks could be assigned to the tetragonal perovskite structure [28] and no obvious changes be found concerning about the crystalline phase and crystallinity. Moreover, it was also displayed little difference of the perovskite morphology among the SEM images in Fig. S4b-d; all of them were full-coverage and uniform with the grain sizes of several hundreds of nanometers.

To explore the effects of GQDs on the performance of mesoscopic PSCs, the devices with and without GQDs decoration were fabricated, respectively. The perovskite solar cells were fabricated with a configuration of Au/spiro-MeOTAD/GQD-x/mp-TiO2/compact TiO2/FTO (x = 0, 1, 2). And the current (J)-voltage (V) characteristics of the solar cells were measured under simulated air mass 1.5 global (AM 1.5G) solar irradiation. As can be seen from Fig. 3 a and Table 1 , the most important observation from the J-V curves was the beneficial role of the tiny GQDs decoration of the mesoporous TiO2 film: the cell with the decoration of GQDs achieved a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 19.20%, mainly contributed to a significant increase of the short-circuit current density from 24.00 to 25.05 mA cm−2 compared with the non-GQDs decoration device, whereas the fill factor (FF) and open-circuit voltages (Voc) were similar. The maximum power point (MPP) tracking shown in Figure. S5 indicates that the stabilized efficiency values of the devices are close to the results obtained from the J-V scan. Besides, this Jsc variation was also confirmed by the incident photo-to-current efficiency (IPCE) measurement, as depicted in Fig. 3b. The devices decorated with GQDs represented higher quantum efficiency in the range of 400–750 nm compared with the non-GQDs decoration cell. The integrated photocurrent values from the IPCE spectra of the GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 were 23.28, 24.07 and 23.28 mA cm−2, the deviations of the corresponding samples are 4.8%, 3.9% and 4.9%, respectively, which are within 5% error compared to the corresponding Jsc obtained from J-V curves. Nevertheless, it also should be noted that all the photovoltaic parameters of PSCs were decreased when the decoration time of GQDs was increased, it was speculated that more recombination centers would be introduced at the interface between ETL and perovskite layer with an increasing amount of GQDs on the surface of the mesoporous TiO2 [23]. Moreover, Figure S6 gives the J-V characteristics of the champion PSC with GQD-1/mp-TiO2 as the ETL which was measured under both reverse and forward scan directions. The PCE achieved by forward scan and reverse scan is 19.70% and 20.45%, respectively. The deviation is only 3.7%. And this negligible hysteresis can be attributed to a better electron transfer at the GQD/mp-TiO2 and perovskite interface.

Fig. S7 shows the box charts concerning about the main photovoltaic parameters’ distribution of 30 PSC devices with the one time decoration of GQDs. It notes that the average Jsc of 24.57 mA cm−2 could be achieved through the introduction of GQDs onto the surface of mp-TiO2 (Fig. S7a). In addition, the averages of 1.06 V for Voc (Fig. S7b) and 0.73 for FF (Fig. S7c) could be obtained as well, yielding an average PCE up to 19% (Fig. S7d). Ultimately, through a series of delicate control of the fabrication process about the GQDs decorated PSC, the best performance of 20.45% (Jsc of 24.92 mA cm−2, Voc of 1.08 V and FF of 0.76) was achieved as shown in Fig. 4 .

From the J-V curves of various cells described above, the enhanced photocurrent was almost the contribution to the superior perovskite solar cell performance obtained through the decoration of the GQDs. To further demonstrate the principle of this phenomenon, a possible model for the decoration of GQDs on the surface of mp-TiO2 is illustrated in Fig. 5 . The cross-sectional SEM of GQDs decorated PSC device is shown in Fig. 5a, from a well fabricated and uniform structure of mesoscopic PSC could be informed. More importantly, as displayed in Fig. 5b, when the GQDs were inserted, they were more likely infiltrated at the poles and cracks of mp-TiO2 film which could be towards to more smooth mesoporous film. And it was beneficial for the perovskite layer contacting with the electron-transported layer and helping extract the electron from the photo-active film. Moreover, due to the superior charge mobility, GQDs might provide improved electrical conductivity of the layer as compared to neat TiO2, besides, here GQDs could also play an important role as an electron acceptor in much the same way as fullerenes do, offering an efficient channel for charge collection and a fast path for transporting electrons originated from photo-excitations on perovskite layer [13]. Alternatively, because of the widely distributed π orbitals acted as a superfast electron funnel [29,30], GQDs could help the electronic coupling between the perovskite layer and mp-TiO2 layer, and an effective bridge could be formed by GQDs decoration that efficiently improved the charge injection from the perovskite to ETL.

Electronic impedance spectroscopy (EIS) characterization in the dark condition was carried out to examine the electronic processes at the perovskite/GQDs decorated ETL interface and to prove the speculation mentioned above. Fig. 6 a depicts the Nyquist plots of the devices with and without the decoration of the GQDs, and the fitted parameters by the equivalent circuit are listed in Table 2 . Typically, Nyquist plots displayed two distinct arcs at low and high frequencies under dark conditions, however, only one arc could be obtained [31,32]. From the EIS measurements, the series resistance in the device (Rs) and the recombination resistance (Rrec) could be extracted. It notes that the series resistance in the cell could be the reduced through the introduction of GQDs (Fig. 6a). The Rs of the reference and optimized device were 11.50 and 8.36 Ω, respectively, inferred that the smaller Rs facilitated the carrier transport and reduced the contact resistance at the perovskite/n-type layer, leading to an enhanced Jsc which was consistent with that extracted from the J-V curves and explained the increase in the photovoltaic performance. Nevertheless, it is also worthy to envisage that the recombination resistances of the cells would be decreased when the GQDs were coated on the surface of the mp-TiO2, while the Rrec explicitly relating to the charge recombination [33]. It is speculated that the highly conductive GQDs should introduce new recombination centers and be disadvantageous for the electron and hole separation in the solar cells [13]. Therefore, to achieve a better photovoltaic performance, an equilibrium process between the series resistance and the recombination rate within the inner system of the GQDs-decorated PSCs should be taken carefully into account.

To demonstrate that the GQDs can enhance the photocurrent effectively and the charge collection efficiency, the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) using GQDs-decorated mesoporous TiO2 and pristine mesoporous TiO2 as the electron transporting layers were prepared. DSSCs were fabricated according to the previously reported procedures [34].

In the DSSCs field, the intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) of the DSSCs based on GQDs decorating were measured to characterize the electron transport and the recombination time. The electron transport time (τd) and recombination time (τr) are obtained from the equation (1):
(1)
τ
d
= 1 / 2 π
f
d
,
τ
r
= 1 / 2 π
f
r
where
f
d
and
f
r
are both the characteristic peak frequency with the minimum value of the IMPS (IMVS) imaginary component. Fig. S8 a and b show the comparison of the key parameters of the time constant for electron transport and electron recombination of the DSSCs based on the controlled device and the GQDs decorated device. The τd and τr are decreased with the light intensity increasing. Furthermore, with the decoration of GQDs, the τd of the DSSC is smaller and the τr is larger under the various light intensities in contrast to the controlled device. The results imply that the electron transport can be accelerated after the GQDs decoration on the mesoporous TiO2, which is consistent with the enhanced photocurrent. In addition, the charge collection efficiency (
η
c c
) was determined according to the following equation (2):
(2)
η
c c
=
1 −
τ
d
/
τ
r

As the curves plotted in Fig. S8c, the DSSCs with the GQDs decoration show higher charge collection efficiency. And the greatly improved charge collection efficiency is the probably reason for the increase of the photocurrent and the superior power conversion efficiency of the devices after the GQDs decoration.

The notion demonstrated above that the electron extraction facilitated by decorating the GQDs was corroborated by the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) measurement. Fig. 7 a shows the steady-state PL spectra of the perovskite films based on GQD-0/mp-TiO2, GQD-1/mp-TiO2 and GQD-2/mp-TiO2 ETLs, respectively: strong emission peaks at ∼780 nm. All GQD decoration led to the PL quenching with respect to the perovskite/FTO film, indicating that it could be caused by the significantly improved charge carrier extraction arising from the insertion of the GQDs. Particularly, perovskite/GQD-1/mp-TiO2 film presented the remarkable quenched emission intensity, indicative of the fastest electron extraction. This result could give the explanation why the photo-excited electrons transported rapidly from the perovskite to the ETL, implying that the electrons passed through the GQDs effectively between the perovskite and mp-TiO2 due to the high conductivity of the graphene materials. The above results elucidate that the GQDs decorated mp-TiO2 films have a higher PL quenching efficiency and quenching rate. Moreover, the PL intensity tendency was also confirmed based on time-resolved PL (TR-PL) excited at 406 nm (Fig. 7b). The TR-PL spectra of the samples were fitted with a bi-exponential decay function as following equation (3) and the corresponding parameters were summarized in Table 3 .
(3)
y fit = y 0 + A 1 exp(-t/τ1) + A 2 exp(-t/τ2)

The fast decay τ1 might be related to the transportation of photo-exited carriers from the photo-active film to the electron-transporting film [35,36] and the long decay component τ2 could be due to the recombination of free carriers in radiative channels [37]. As could be observed, the PL decay of the perovskite deposited on pristine mesoporous TiO2 exhibits a time of τ1 = 59.9 ns, whereas τ1 = 396.6 ns for the reference one. The much faster decay time for the perovskite/GQD-0/mp-TiO2 substrate suggested an efficient charge extraction from (FAPbI3)1-x (MAPbBr3)x film to the ETL. In addition, it is also discovered that a faster PL decay (τ1 = 49.5 ns) could be observed for the perovskite/GQD-1/mp-TiO2 substrate. It means that inserting GQDs into mesoporous TiO2 film could strengthen the charge extraction ability at the perovskite/ETL interface. Therefore, the Jsc was obviously increased. However, the PL decay (τ1 = 55.2 ns) was increased for the perovskite/GQD-2/mp-TiO2 compared to that of perovskite/GQD-1/mp-TiO2, indicating that an excess GQDs might not be benefit to the electron transport due to the increase of the recombination rate, at same time, this would no advantageous for the devices’ performance.

In summary, high-efficiency (>20%) perovskite solar cells have been designed by decorating tiny GQDs (<5 nm) on the surface of the mesoporous TiO2 with a facile method. With optimized GQDs decoration time and the delicate control of the PSC fabrication, an impressive PCE of 20.45% can be achieved, and this efficiency is relatively higher than that of the 18.57% efficiency without the GQDs decoration. Furthermore, the EIS measurement proves that the decoration of GQDs induce the reduction of the series resistance of the device by reducing the contact resistance of the perovskite/mp-TiO2 interface. Hence, the enhancement of photovoltaic performance is mostly ascribable to the promotion of the photocurrent. Moreover, by virtue of the steady-state and time-resolved PL measurements, it is highlighted that the beneficial role of GQDs on the surface of mp-TiO2 film is to facilitate the electron extraction and the transportation from the perovskite absorber to the current collector, other than the pristine mp-TiO2, and thus provide a reasonable explanation to the performance enhancement of the GQD decorated cell. Consequently, the present work offers a simple and effective approach as well as a useful strategy for enhancing the efficiency of the perovskite solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1505241, 91433104, 21875041 and 21750110442) and Fujian Provincial Department of Science and Technology (2015J05022).
